title: Eggs Benedict
subtitle: Poached eggs and back bacon on an English muffin with hollandaise
excerpt: The American brunch landmark: a split English muffin toasted and buttered, topped with a round of back bacon or Canadian bacon, a perfectly poached egg, and a generous spoonful of warm hollandaise. The hollandaise is the technical part; everything else is assembly.
sourceNotes: Eggs Benedict appears in Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544), where she includes both the poached egg method and a hollandaise preparation. The hollandaise sauce is documented in Auguste Escoffier's 'Le Guide Culinaire' (1903, first English translation 1907). The dish itself is attributed to either Charles Ranhofer at Delmonico's restaurant in New York (1894) or to a New York City stockbroker named Lemuel Benedict who invented it at the Waldorf Hotel (1894).

glossaryTerms.definition: A warm emulsion sauce made from egg yolks, clarified butter, and lemon juice, whisked over gentle heat until thick and smooth. One of the five French mother sauces.

content.text: Eggs Benedict has a reputation for being difficult that it does not entirely deserve. The poached egg requires practice but follows consistent rules. The

requires heat control but comes together in 10 minutes once you understand the temperature. The rest (toasting a muffin, frying a round of bacon) is straightforward. The dish rewards organisation: make the hollandaise first, keep it warm, then poach the eggs at the last moment.

A fresh egg poaches far more tidily than an older egg; the white clings around the yolk rather than spreading into wispy threads. A splash of cider vinegar in the poaching water helps the white set faster. The yolk should wobble when the egg is lifted out — if it is firm, it is overcooked.

Put the {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks and a tablespoon of the lemon juice in a heatproof metal bowl. Set the bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water (the base of the bowl should not touch the water). Whisk constantly for 3 to 4 minutes until the mixture is pale, thick, and leaves a ribbon trail on the surface when you lift the whisk. Remove from the heat. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter in a thin, steady stream, whisking constantly. The mixture should thicken to a smooth, glossy sauce. Season with salt, cayenne, and more lemon juice to taste. Keep warm off the heat.
Cook the bacon and toast the muffins
Fry the back bacon rounds in a dry pan over medium heat for 2 minutes per side until lightly golden. Toast the split English muffins until golden. Keep warm.

Bring a wide saucepan of water to a gentle simmer — not a rolling boil. Add the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar. Crack each egg into a small cup first. Create a gentle swirl in the water with a spoon, then slide the egg into the centre. Poach for 3 minutes for a runny yolk. Lift with a slotted spoon and rest briefly on kitchen paper. To assemble: muffin, bacon round, poached egg, a generous spoonful of hollandaise, a pinch of cayenne.

items.cause: Heat too high; egg yolks cooked rather than gently warmed
items.fix: The water under the bowl should barely simmer; if the sauce starts to look grainy, remove immediately from heat and whisk in an ice cube or a tablespoon of cold water to drop the temperature

items.symptom: Poached egg has wispy white threads
items.cause: Egg not fresh enough, or water at a rolling boil rather than a simmer
items.fix: Use the freshest eggs available; keep the water at a gentle simmer (small bubbles, not aggressive) and add vinegar, which speeds white coagulation

items.cause: Butter added too quickly, or sauce reduced too much over heat
items.fix: Whisk in a teaspoon of warm water at a time until the sauce loosens to a pourable but not runny consistency

items.symptom: Eggs are overdone by the time hollandaise is made
items.cause: Timing not coordinated; hollandaise should always be made first
items.fix: Make the hollandaise first and keep it warm in the bowl off the heat; poach the eggs last so they are served immediately

content.text: Eggs Benedict arrived at New York's Delmonico's or the Waldorf Hotel in the 1890s, depending on which version of the story you prefer, and became the defining American brunch dish of the twentieth century. Brunch itself as a social institution took hold in New York and other American cities in the 1930s and 1940s, and Eggs Benedict was the dish that gave it a focal point. The combination of egg, ham, bread, and sauce is so clearly sensible that the dish would have been invented somewhere eventually; what the Americans added was the occasion to eat it: the long Sunday morning, the second coffee, the newspaper.
